Name: rate_distortion_neural_networks
Title: Information-Theoretic Bounds for Neural Network Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: Neural network compression can be formulated as a rate-distortion problem, where the goal is to minimize the number of bits required to represent the network while maintaining its predictive performance. We hypothesize that information-theoretic bounds can be derived to characterize the fundamental tradeoff between compression rate and distortion in neural networks.
Related Work: While there have been numerous efforts to compress neural networks using various techniques such as quantization and pruning, the majority of these approaches lack a rigorous information-theoretic foundation. Our proposal seeks to fill this gap by developing a rate-distortion framework for neural network compression, drawing inspiration from classical source coding theory.
Abstract: This project aims to establish a theoretical foundation for neural network compression by formulating it as a rate-distortion problem. We will derive information-theoretic bounds on the minimum number of bits required to represent a neural network while maintaining its predictive performance. Our approach will leverage tools from source coding theory, such as rate-distortion theory and the information bottleneck method. The resulting framework will provide a principled way to evaluate and optimize neural network compression algorithms, with potential applications in edge AI, model deployment, and efficient training.
Experiments: Derive achievable rate-distortion bounds for neural networks using techniques from source coding theory
Develop a rate-distortion framework for evaluating and optimizing neural network compression algorithms
Conduct experiments to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in compressing neural networks while maintaining their predictive performance
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of neural networks may render the derivation of tight information-theoretic bounds challenging
The development of practical compression algorithms that achieve the derived bounds may require significant additional effort
